items.mistake: Differentiating with respect to x without treating y (and other variables) as constants
items.fix: When computing ∂f/∂x, freeze every variable except x and apply ordinary derivative rules.
items.why: Treating y as a function of x when it is an independent variable produces wrong partials and wrong gradients.
items.example: For f(x, y) = x²y, ∂f/∂x = 2xy (not 2xy + x²·dy/dx); y is held constant so only the x² factor differentiates.

items.mistake: Confusing partial derivative notation ∂f/∂x with total derivative df/dx when other variables depend on x
items.fix: Use partials when variables are independent; use the chain rule (total derivative) when y = g(x) or parameters are linked.
items.why: In ML, weights are usually independent optimization variables—partials apply—but composed graphs need the chain rule on top.
items.example: If L(w, b) treats w and b as independent, ∂L/∂w ignores b; if b = 2w, you must use dL/dw = ∂L/∂w + (∂L/∂b)(db/dw).

items.mistake: Assuming ∂f/∂x = 0 whenever f does not visibly contain x
items.fix: Check whether x appears inside composed functions (e.g. f(g(x), y)); use the chain rule.
items.why: Hidden dependence through intermediates is the norm in neural networks—every layer couples variables.
items.example: For f(x, y) = sin(xy), ∂f/∂x = y·cos(xy), not 0, even though x is not a bare additive term.
